coredata.dc:description: To satisfy the urgent demand to green, renewable and high value-added building and decorative materials, the research field on photoluminescent transparent wood has been developed more and more rapidly. Herein, approximately 84.7 % of lignin in balsa wood was modified to remove the chromogenic groups, and approximately 15.3 % of lignin was removed, thereby exposing more hydroxyl groups and pores of wood. Such pretreated balsa wood was then cross-linked with polyvinyl alcohol to create transparent wood films (TWF). By impregnating the treated wood with polyvinyl alcohol and blue or yellow carbon dots (BCDs or YCDs), blue and yellow photoluminescent TWFs (B-TWF and Y-TWF) were produced. The B-TWF incorporates BCDs throughout the wood following the polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), while Y-TWF has YCDs cross-linked with lignocellulose in the wood cell walls initially and then PVA in wood cell cavities. Both B-TWF and Y-TWF exhibited excellent light scattering and flexibility. Y-TWF had higher UV-blocking rate (92.4 %) and tensile strength (39.5 MPa) but lower visible light transmittance (64.8 %) compared to B-TWF (82.5 %, 22.7 MPa and 65.8 %), as well as good expansibility and water stability, which used to overlay a glass window indoors append additional resistance to UV and light scattering.

Irradiance count value and UV-blocking rate of (d) B-TWF and (e) Y-TWF. (f) Physical photos of UV-sensitive cards under various conditions. Color differences of (g) B-TWF with various ratios and (h) Y-TWF with various impregnation time.

The insets show the optical photographs of Y-TWF samples after treatment under visible and UV light. Changes of Y-TWF samples in (e) mass, (f) tensile strength and (g) UV resistance after treatment at different relative humidities. (h) Optical photographs of Y-TWF under visible and UV light in original, swelling and recovery states. (i) Tensile strength variation of Y-TWF after swelling and recovery.

head.ce:title: Preparation of photoluminescent transparent balsa wood with densifying structure for light-scattering windows based on cross-linking of lignocellulose and poly(vinyl alcohol)

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Wood Material Science and Engineering Key Laboratory of Jilin Province, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Beihua University, Jilin 132013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Wood Material Science and Engineering Key Laboratory of Jilin Province
School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Wood Material Science and Engineering Key Laboratory of Jilin Province, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Beihua University, Jilin 132013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To satisfy the urgent demand to green, renewable and high value-added building and decorative materials, the research field on photoluminescent transparent wood has been developed more and more rapidly. Herein, approximately 84.7 % of lignin in balsa wood was modified to remove the chromogenic groups, and approximately 15.3 % of lignin was removed, thereby exposing more hydroxyl groups and pores of wood. Such pretreated balsa wood was then cross-linked with polyvinyl alcohol to create transparent wood films (TWF). By impregnating the treated wood with polyvinyl alcohol and blue or yellow carbon dots (BCDs or YCDs), blue and yellow photoluminescent TWFs (B-TWF and Y-TWF) were produced. The B-TWF incorporates BCDs throughout the wood following the polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), while Y-TWF has YCDs cross-linked with lignocellulose in the wood cell walls initially and then PVA in wood cell cavities. Both B-TWF and Y-TWF exhibited excellent light scattering and flexibility. Y-TWF had higher UV-blocking rate (92.4 %) and tensile strength (39.5 MPa) but lower visible light transmittance (64.8 %) compared to B-TWF (82.5 %, 22.7 MPa and 65.8 %), as well as good expansibility and water stability, which used to overlay a glass window indoors append additional resistance to UV and light scattering.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this published article.

ce:section.ce:para: As increasingly serious global environmental problems, the demand for environmentally friendly materials is growing. Wood is one of the representatives of environmentally friendly materials due to its renewable [1] and biodegradable [2,3] properties. The use of wood not only reduces dependence on fossil resources but also can be naturally decomposed at the end of its life cycle with less impact on the environment [4,5]. Therefore, wood is widely used as the first choice of environmentally friendly materials in many fields such as construction, furniture, and packaging [6]. The functionalization of wood is an important research direction in modern materials science [7]. The functionalization of wood not only enhances its practical utility but also broadens the scope of its potential applications such as superhydrophobicity [8], flame retardancy [9,10], antimicrobial activity [11], and self-cleaning capabilities [12]. Nevertheless, among the many functionalized kinds of wood, fluorescent transparent wood is attracting attention due to its unique optical properties.

To realize wood transparency, lignin and other color-generating substances in wood must be removed or modified to form the wood template, the internal pores of which were fully filled with the resin with similar refractive index [13,14]. The preparation of fluorescent transparent wood needs the incorporation of suitable fluorescent materials during the manufacturing process. The combination of fluorescent materials with wood allows the wood to emit fluorescence under specific light conditions [15]. By combining fluorescent materials with wood, which have emerged as a promising avenue of research due to their high sensitivity to fluorescence, extensive range of material sources, and environmentally friendly characteristics. For example, they can be applied to decorative objects [15], lighting fixtures [16], or safety signs [17] with special visual effects, thereby introducing innovative visual elements to interior design and architecture. Furthermore, they can be utilized to provide illumination in nocturnal or low-light settings, thereby enhancing the functionality of applications in emergency scenarios [16]. Despite some successes in the field of fluorescent transparent wood, further improvements are still necessary. Firstly, it is essential to enhance the stability of the fluorescent material within the wood in order to guarantee the long-term reliability of the fluorescent properties. Secondly, the production process requires further optimization in order to reduce production costs and enhance production efficiency. Moreover, the environmental impact of the production process must be reduced in order to enhance the sustainability of the product. For instance, carbon dots (CDs) are synthesized from biomass (e.g., citric acid), which were encapsulated in environmentally friendly resins (e.g., polyvinyl alcohol) and wood to ensure the fluorescence stability, environmental friendliness and low cost of product.

Currently, the wood veneers are bleached by the removal of lignin, and then impregnated with CDs and resins, which could be encapsulated into the delignified wood veneers. This process resulted in the preparation of fluorescent transparent wood films with photoluminescent properties, flexibility, and excellent mechanical properties. This renders fluorescent transparent wood a multifunctional material with a wide range of applications in numerous fields. Zou et al. [18] developed photoluminescent quantum dots/ultra-thin transparent wood films with excellent flexibility and optical properties, which were prepared for application in light-emitting diodes. Liu et al. [19] encapsulated lignin-derived CDs and polyvinyl alcohol into delignified wood templates, which were placed in a formaldehyde-containing house to emit fluorescence for real-time formaldehyde monitoring. Höglund et al. [20] employed the cell wall structure of delignified wood, comprising nanoporous cellulose with a high specific surface area, to desorb a high concentration of unaggregated rhodamine 6G. This was then applied to a laser-emitting material, resulting in the emission of a bright beam of light. Yang et al. [21] introduced lanthanide ions to delignifed wood (wood-derived cellulose) modified with citric acid for fabricating cellulose-based fluorescent composites membrane, displaying the significant potential in anti-counterfeiting application [22]. The wood veneers were subjected to a bleaching process, which involved the removal of the lignin component. This was followed by the impregnation of fluorescent materials and resins, resulting in the production of fluorescent transparent wood that exhibited photoluminescent properties, flexibility, and excellent mechanical properties. The encapsulation of fluorescent materials has the potential to greatly expand the range of applications for transparent wood films.

Carbon dots possess numbers of merits including wide range of sources, low toxicity, controlled fluorescence, and good biocompatibility [23,24]. Many efforts have been focused on using renewable natural resources as carbon sources to prepare CDs, such as lignin [23], cyanobacteria [24], pomelo peel [25], sugarcane waste [26], tobacco [27], etc. Except for the most basic constituent elements (C, H, O), the doping elements (N, S, P) are usually introduced into CDs [28], which can enhance the fluorescence of CDs. For instance, the fluorescence quantum yield of N-doped CDs was higher, and the corresponding emission light was red-shifted [29]. Furthermore, the doping elements can also modify the electronic structure of CDs [30], and enhance the chemical stability of CDs [31]. Herein, N-doped CDs with blue fluorescence (BCDs with fluorescence quantum yield of 49 %) were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis method using citric acid as carbon source and urea as nitrogen dopant [32]. In low-temperature heating method, N-doped CDs with yellow fluorescence (YCDs with fluorescence quantum yield of 77 %) were prepared using o-phenylenediamine as the carbon source without additional dopants, which contains high nitrogen content [33]. Afterwards, as-prepared BCDs and YCDs are encapsulated inside the wood veneers after lignin modifying pretreatment with the introduction of PVA to obtain the fluorescent transparent wood films (B-TWF and Y-TWF) with densifying structure in natural air-drying method. This work offers a novel, simple, low-energy and self-densifying approach to the wood products for the applications in building and decorative materials with unique light scattering, transparency, photoluminescence, UV resistance, etc.

The balsa wood (30 × 30 × 1 mm3) was procured from a local factory (Jilin Province, China). The following reagents were purchased from Tianjin Zhiyuan Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd.: sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96 wt%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%), o-phenylenediamine (o-PDA, 98.5 wt%), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4, 99 wt%), and disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (Na2EDTA, 99 wt%). The urea (CO(NH2)2, 99.5 wt%) was procured from Yongfeng Biotechnology Co. Ltd. Anhydrous ethanol (C2H6O, 99.7 wt%) was procured from Tianjin Damaoxue Reagent Co. Ltd. Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7.H2O, CA, 99 wt%) was procured from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, [-CH2CHOH-], 97 wt%) was procured from Wuxi Yatai United Chemical Co. Ltd. Deionized water was prepared in the laboratory. All reagents utilized in the experiment were employed in their original state without any additional purification.

In this work, the pretreatment is a process of lignin modification in wood. Lignin-modified wood was prepared according to previous experimental methods [15]. The wood veneers (Nature Wood, NW) were obtained through rotary cutting of balsa wood. A mixed aqueous solution of NaOH (3 wt%) and Na2EDTA (0.1 wt%) was prepared and heated at 70 °C for 10 min. Once dissolution was complete, MgSO4 (0.1 wt%) and H2O2 (4.0 wt%) were added to the mixed solution. The NW was then immersed in the mixed solution and heated at 70 °C for 6 h. The wood veneers were whitened completely, and the prepared lignin-retained wood (LW) was transferred to deionized water to remove residual chemicals. It is important to note that the water-washed LW is stored in a mixture of anhydrous ethanol and acetone for a period exceeding 12 h in order to remove residual moisture.

As presented in Fig. S1 in Supplementary Material, the main chromogenic groups in wood, which typically stem from the lignin molecule (containing aromatic ketones and coniferyl aldehyde-like structures) [34], were modified with hydrogen peroxide. This process resulted in the chromogenic groups on the lignin being shed, while the modified lignin was retained in the wood. The lignin contents in original wood and LW have been measured in accordance with GB/T 2677.8–9. As presented in Fig. S2 in Supplementary Material, the lignin content in original wood and LW were 26.1 wt% and 22.1 wt%, respectively. Apparently, most lignin (84.7 wt%) was modified, and a small amount of lignin (15.3 wt%) will be removed. Such modification process could effectively remove the chromogenic groups in lignin, resulting in a white appearance for the LW. The L* (luminance), a* (red/green) and b* (yellow/blue) values of LW were recorded by the L*-a*-b* colorimetric system. The L*, a* and b* values of LW were 85.32, 2.67 and 5.68, respectively.

Preparation of blue carbon dots (BCDs) was based on previous studies (Fig. 2(a)) [32]. The synthesis of BCDs was achieved via a hydrothermal method. The synthesis was conducted by combining CA (2.5 wt%) and urea (5 wt%) under magnetic stirring condition at 30 r/s for 10 min. Once the solids had been dissolved, the mixed solution was placed in a high-temperature and high-pressure reactor, which was subjected to a thermal treatment at 160 °C for 6 h. Upon completion of the thermal treatment, the reactor was cooled, and the centrifugation was performed to the product at a rotational speed of 6000 r/s for 15 min. Then the supernatant was collected and extracted to obtain the resulting BCDs solution. Subsequently, the resulting BCDs solution underwent a 72-h dialysis process (molecular weight: 1000 Da). Following the further freeze-drying, solid BCDs were collected and weighed. BCDs solutions with the concentrations of 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 mg/mL utilized in the subsequent experiments were prepared from the solid BCDs and deionized water.

Preparation of yellow carbon dots (YCDs) was based on previous studies (Fig. 3(a)) [33]. The YCDs were synthesized through the low-temperature heating of o-PDA. Initially, o-PDA (5 wt%) was placed into a beaker and stirred at 30 r/s for 2 h at 60 °C. Subsequently, a small quantity of CA was added to the beaker, and the pH was adjusted to a range of 5–6. The mixture was then stirred for 10 h at 60 °C with a speed of 30 r/s. Once the temperature had been reduced to room temperature, the hydrophobic YCDs were obtained by filtration through a vacuum filtration device. The resulting powder was collected and stored. Then the YCDs solution (10 mg/mL) were prepared in ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent transparent wood film
ce:section.ce:para: PVA (10 wt%) solution was prepared under stirring condition (30 r/s) at 80 °C for 2 h. Then LW samples were impregnated with PVA solution for 2 h, and transferred to the slides. After drying treatment, the transparent wood films (TWF) were obtained. In this study, we selected natural air-drying method to prepare the TWF samples, which would save a lot of production cost. Notably, if the PVA film was formed solely on the surface of LW, the light transmittance of product would be very poor as presented in Fig. S3(a) in Supplementary Material. Only totally completing the cross-linking between PVA and cellulose in LW could create the TWF with high light transmission (Fig. S3(b) in Supplementary Material). The L*, a* and b* of TWF were measured with a white A4 paper under it by the L*-a*-b* colorimetric system as well. The values were 81.89, 0.56 and 0.32, respectively.

The preparation process has been depicted in Fig. 1 . PVA, BCDs, and deionized water were combined in accordance with a mass ratio of 100:x:1000 (where x = 1, 2, 3, 4 or 5), and heated at 80 °C at a rate of 30 r/s for 2 h. The LW was impregnated in the mixed solution for 2 h, and then the LW was placed on the slide and air-drying for 12 h to obtain the blue fluorescent transparent wood film (B-TWF), which was named according to the content of BCDs, respectively. The following abbreviations are used throughout this study: B-TWF-1, B-TWF-2, B-TWF-3, B-TWF-4, and B-TWF-5. The LW was placed in YCDs solution and impregnated for 5, 10, 15, 20 and 25 min. Afterwards, the LW impregnated with YCDs (Y-LW) was washed three times with deionized water to remove the unstable YCDs adsorbed on the LW surface. The Y-LW was then placed in PVA solution for 2 h, which was further placed on slide. After natural air-drying for 12 h, a yellow fluorescent transparent wood film (Y-TWF) was obtained.

As provided in Fig. S4 in Supplementary Material, the Y-LW still remained wet after 18 h of air-drying with assisted vacuum. Although the assisted vacuum condition was favorable for PVA impregnation in Y-LW, the lack of pressure and air circulation resulted in a low water evaporation rate. Therefore, Y-TWF was still not formed after 24 h of air-drying with assisted vacuum. However, Y-TWF could be successfully formed only after 12 h of natural air-drying. Apparently, natural air-drying method could facilitate the formation of Y-TWF from Y-LW filling with PVA solution rapidly. These samples were named according to the impregnation time with the following designations of Y-TWF-5, Y-TWF-10, Y-TWF-15, Y-TWF-20 and Y-TWF-25. Unless otherwise noted, B-TWF indicates B-TWF-3, and Y-TWF indicates Y-TWF-15.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods for testing and characterizing carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical composition of the samples was analyzed using a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Bruker, Germany, Tensor 27). The blue and yellow CDs powders were completely dried in an oven and then mixed with spectroscopically pure potassium bromide at a mass ratio of 1:1000, respectively, and then completely milled. The samples were pressed by the pressing method and then placed into the instrument with the test range set at 500–4000 cm−1 for infrared testing.

The UV–visible spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan, U-3900) was employed to quantify the UV absorbance of the purified CDs solution. The samples were introduced into the instrument with the test range of UV absorption set at 200–800 nm, within the instrument's designated wavelength range.

The excitation and emission spectra of the purified CDs were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, USA, Cary Eclipse). The excitation light was set to 365 nm, the emission spectrum was tested, and the emission peak
λ max
of the emission spectrum was identified. Subsequently, the excitation spectrum was tested using the identified
λ max
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods for testing and characterizing Fl-TWF
ce:section.ce:para:
The density of the samples was measured and calculated using an electronic balance and vernier calipers. The density was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
ρ =
m V
where ρ is the density, m is the mass, and V is the volume.

The chemical composition of the samples was analyzed using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Bruker, Germany, Tensor 27). TWF, B-TWF and Y-TWF were ground to powder and completely dried in an oven. They were then mixed with spectroscopically pure potassium bromide at a mass ratio of 1:1000, respectively, and then completely ground and pressed into tablets. For the purposes of infrared testing, the testing range should be set from 500 to 4000 cm−1.

To ascertain the water contact angle (WCA) of the samples, a contact angle tester (Shanghai Zhongchen, Model JC2000C) was employed. The WCAs of the following samples were measured: NW, LW, PVA, TWF, B-TWF, and Y-TWF, in that order.

In order to analyze the wettability of the samples, they were immersed in pure water. The mass was quantified over time, and the wettability was evaluated. The degree of swelling was calculated using the following formula:
(2)
Swelling =
m 0
− m
m 0
× 100 %
where
m 0
is the initial mass and m is the mass after swelling.

The mechanical testing machine (Dongguan Zhitao, model ZQ-990 A) was employed to assess the mechanical properties of stress and strain. The scale distance of the mechanical testing machine was calibrated to 10 mm, the speed was set at 30 mm/min, and the Young's modulus of the samples was calculated using the following formula:
(3)
E =
σ ε
where E is the modulus of elasticity, σ is the applied stress and ε is the strain.

The color difference (e.g. L*-luminance, a*-red/green and b*-yellow/blue values) of various samples was quantified by using a precision colorimeter (Shenzhen Sann, Model SC-10+) containing the L*-a*-b* colorimetric system.

The UV-blocking rate was measured using a Tenmar's TM-213 instrument with a UV lamp power of 50 W. The tester was placed at a distance of 7 cm from the UV lamp and the resulting values were calculated using the following formula:
(4)
η =
E 0
− E
E 0
× 100 %
where η is the UV barrier rate,
E 0
is the value obtained when the irradiator probe is exposed to UV light without shielding, and E is the value obtained when the various samples are shielded.

The transmittance of TWF, B-TWF, and Y-TWF was evaluated through a solid-state UV test (UV 3600 Plus, Shimadzu, Japan) across a wavelength range of 200–800 nm. The photoaging experiments of Fl-TWF samples were carried out in an ultraviolet aging chamber (ST-WK80A, Kunshan, China) with a wavelength range of 315–400 nm, and a power of 2000 W.

As illustrated in Fig. 2(b), the BCDs are constituted of C, O, and N with the elemental percentages of 75.96 %, 21.45 % and 2.59 %, respectively. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2(c)) reveals that 284.8 eV is associated with C-C/C=C [35,36], 285.7 eV with CN [37], and 288.2 eV with C-O/C=O [38]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2(d)) indicates that 531.4 eV is -OH [39] and 532.4 eV is CO [36], which suggests that a small amount of carboxylic acid groups are also present in BCDs. Fig. 2(e) depicts the N 1 s spectrum, wherein 399.8 eV is attributed to pyridinic N and 400.7 eV to pyrrolic N [40]. Consequently, BCDs have been successfully doped with N, and they possess a hydrophilic functional group, namely -COOH. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the YCDs are composed of C, O, and N with the elemental percentages of 79.52 %, 11.67 %, and 8.81 %, respectively. Among them, the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3(c)) shows 284.1 eV for C-C/C=C, 285.8 eV for C-N/C-O, and 288.0 eV for C-N/C-O [41,42]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3(d)) has 531.3 eV corresponding to C-O [39] and 532.8 eV for CO [36]. The 399.3 and 398.1 eV in the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3(e)) are attributed to the pyrrolic and pyridinic N [43,44], respectively.

The absorption and fluorescence spectra of the BCDs solution are shown in Fig. 2(f). BCDs have strong absorption throughout the ultraviolet band with the strongest light absorption at 379 nm. Because BCDs contain a large number of conjugated structures, the π → π* leaps of CC bonds and the n → π* leaps of CO bonds at the UV wavelengths [23], which results in the enhanced UV-blocking capability of B-TWF. There are two strong excitation wavelengths in the excitation spectra of BCDs at 316 nm and 348 nm, indicating that BCDs can convert the energy absorbed in the UV to the energy required to emit fluorescence [45,46]. Consequently, the emission spectrum reaches its maximum intensity at 432 nm, at which point the BCDs solution exhibits a blue fluorescence (as shown in the inset of Fig. 2(g)). However, the solution under visible light appears to be a yellowish-green color as shown in the inset of Fig. 2(f). In Fig. 2(g), the blue fluorescence of BCDs has CIE coordinates (0.167, 0.174) on the colorimetric axis. As illustrated in Fig. 3(f), the absorption spectrum of the YCDs solution exhibits two absorption peaks at 297 and 429 nm, indicating that the CDs predominantly absorb UV-B and blue-violet bands. It also stems from the conjugated structure of YCDs, while its conjugation number is lower than BCDs, thus showing the lower absorption bandwidth. Furthermore, they can convert blue-violet bands into the energy required for the emission of fluorescence. The excitation spectrum has a peak at 432 nm, while the emission spectrum has a peak at 536 nm, which ultimately emits yellow fluorescence (as shown in the inset of Fig. 3(g)), the corresponding CIE coordinates are (0.354, 0.603) (Fig. 3(g)). Meanwhile, YCDs solution appear brownish-yellow in visible light (as shown in the inset of Fig. 3(f)).

As illustrated in Fig. 4(a), the wood after lignin-modified treatment retained the pore structure of cell, but exhibited a uniform white discoloration throughout. Following a washing process with deionized water, the LW is further immersed in anhydrous ethanol. After completing dehydration, the LW was immersed in PVA solution, and subsequently naturally air-drying to obtain the transparent wood film (TWF). During the drying procedure, the wood cells would shrink due to the water loss of PVA solution in LW. This is because PVA and cellulose could produce hydrogen bonding, which would result in the aggregation of cellulose (main compound of cell wall for LW) during the aggregation process of PVA. It demonstrated the cell structure had been compressed and fully filled with PVA. The good compatibility between PVA and cellulose resulted in the excellent light transmittance of TWF. With good water-soluble property, BCDs were mixed to the PVA solution, which further impregnated the LW for the preparation of B-TWF. In contrast, the oil-soluble YCDs were insoluble in PVA solution, which have to mix to ethanol and further impregnated the LW. Subsequently, the excess YCDs on LW surface, which were unstable in their binding with cellulose, were removed by ethanol rinsing. Finally, it was impregnated with PVA solution for the preparation of Y-TWF. The simulation diagram in Fig. 4(b) illustrated the clear distinction between B-TWF and Y-TWF. Since BCDs were encapsulated in PVA, there was no discernible difference between B-TWF and TWF in terms of tensile and wetting properties. In contrast, YCDs were adsorbed on the LW, resulting from the cross-linking between YCDs and cellulose. The PVA interspersed in LW could form hydrogen bond with cellulose, resulting in the fluorescent transparent wood film (Fl-TWF) with enhanced tensile properties.

Fig. 4(c) showed the cell structure of balsa wood after lignin modification. The flocculent thinner cell wall and the numerous minute apertures on cell wall were attributed to the partial lignin and hemicellulose detachment of wood. Additionally, the stabilized fiber structure became looser, which would facilitate the subsequent impregnation of PVA. To more accurately observe the wood structure variation after PVA impregnation, ultrathin lignin-modified wood veneers were prepared. PVA impregnated the wood veneer one side, and the stabilized microstructure of its other side was observed. The SEM image of TWF confirmed above inference that the aggregation of PVA drove the distortion of lignin-modified wood cell wall, which led to a change in the dimensions of the wood veneers. The SEM image of B-TWF demonstrated a similar effect to the TWF, whereby the cell walls intertangled and shrunk due to the aggregation of PVA, resulting in an increase in wood density. Despite the initial YCDs impregnation and subsequent introduction of PVA, in the SEM image of Y-TWF, the cell walls were still observed to crimp due to the aggregation of PVA, ultimately resulting in the formation of a dense structure. A similar conclusion can be drawn from their SEM images at the fiber direction (Fig. 4(d)). As illustrated in Fig. 5(b), the cross-linking in B-TWF is predominantly a hydrogen bonding between PVA and cellulose. Since BCDs contained the hydrophilic group -COOH, which were first dispersed in PVA solution, BCDs were mainly hydrogen-bonded with PVA and marginally hydrogen-bonded with cellulose. Y-TWF involved the cross-linking of YCDs and cellulose first, followed by the cross-linking of PVA, cellulose and YCDs. This is due to the cross-linking of YCDs among multiple cellulose molecules, after which the PVA chains were interspersed and cross-linked with the cellulose molecules carrying a very small amount of YCDs. As illustrated in Fig. 4(e), when PVA combined with cellulose and filled cavities completely, the wood cell structure became blurred, indicating their high compatibility. From the fluorescence microscope (Fig. S5 in Supplementary Material), it could be found that the contour of wood cell wall after combining with YCDs and PVA solution was blurry. It indicated that YCDs had distributed in the cell wall and cell cavity of wood uniformly, emitting yellow fluorescence throughout the Y-TWF sample.

From Fig. 5(a), we found that the thickness of NW was 1 mm, whereas it turned 0.15 mm after cross-linking with PVA and YCDs, displaying a densified structure. As illustrated in Fig. 5(c), NW was initially subjected to drying at a temperature of 60 °C for 24 h. The resulting density of NW was 0.1516 g·cm−3. The lignin modification method removes some lignin from the NW, resulting in more tiny pores in as-prepared LW. Freeze-drying method ensured the LW as a whole without deformation. The density of the freeze-dried LW was 0.0924 g·cm−3, which makes the impregnation of PVA easier. The density of PVA is 1.3889 g·cm−3, while that of TWF is 1.101 g·cm−3. The cross-linking of PVA and cellulose results in the filling of the pores in the LW, which significantly increases the density from the LW to the TWF, and renders it transparent. The density of B-TWF is 1.2116 g·cm−3. This material contains a large number of hydrophilic functional groups, which are well-compatible with PVA. The density of Y-TWF is 1.0549 g·cm−3, which is a decrease in density compared with TWF, this indicates that the hydrophobic functional groups in the YCDs successfully cross-link with the cellulose chains, which results in a reduction in the number of cross-linking nodes of the PVA with the cellulose chains. This is evidenced by a reduction in density on a macroscopic scale. Nevertheless, Y-TWF retains high light transmission and strong tensile properties.

The LW was reduced to a size of 20 × 20 × 1 mm3. It was oriented with the “Length” dimension aligned with the fiber growth direction, “Wide” dimension perpendicular to the fiber growth direction, and “Thickness” dimension representing the cross-section. As illustrated in Fig. 5(d), the lengths of air-drying TWF, B-TWF, and Y-TWF in the fiber growth direction are relatively stable and minimally affected by PVA aggregation. The lengths are 19.72, 19.89, and 19.94 mm, respectively, exhibiting only a slight reduction. However, the change in width is more pronounced. The widths of TWF and B-TWF are 12.97 mm and 13.46 mm, respectively, representing a decrease of 35.15 % and 32.7 %, respectively. The BCDs exert a diminished influence when the PVA aggregation is subjected to tension by the cellulose. In contrast, the width of Y-TWF is 16.54 mm, with a smaller decrease of 17.3 %. This provides further evidence that the cross-linking of YCDs and cellulose results in the fewer cross-linking nodes between PVA and cellulose chains, which in turn leads to a decrease in density. The thickness of balsa wood with a thickness of 1 mm will be reduced to 0.15 mm after lignin modification and impregnation with YCDs and PVA. This is due to the aggregation of PVA in the process of water evaporation, which leads to the formation of hydrogen bonds between PVA and hydroxyl groups on the cellulose. This aggregation process results in the aggregation of the cellulose together, which ultimately leads to a reduction in the thickness of the TWF. The thicknesses of TWF, B-TWF and Y-TWF are approximately 0.15 mm. Although the structures of B-TWF and Y-TWF are distinct, the larger holes in the cross-section of B-TWF and Y-TWF result in a reduced influence of CDs on the aggregation process. In conclusion, the incorporation of BCDs exerts a relatively minor impact on the density of TWFs. This is because the -COOH group in BCDs is more likely to form hydrogen bonds with PVA, while only a few of these groups are capable of hydrogen bonding with cellulose on the cell wall. Consequently, the structural integrity of TWFs is not significantly affected, and the resulting appearance and morphology remain unaltered. Nevertheless, Y-TWF involves the combination of YCDs with cellulose, followed by the incorporation of PVA, which serves to reinforce the overall cross-linking process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tensile and wetting properties of Fl-TWF
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 6(a), the tensile strength of NW is 2.8 MPa, whereas the lyophilized LW exhibits an inferior tensile strength of 1.5 MPa. This is attributed to the modification and partial removal of lignin, which results in a loose structure [47], thereby facilitating the impregnation of PVA. The tensile strength of PVA is 1.6 MPa, while the elongation at break can reach up to 12.8 %. The tensile strength of TWF was 22.2 MPa. The crosslinking of PVA with cellulose resulted in a notable enhancement in the overall tensile properties [48], as evidenced by an elongation at break of 12.4 %. This phenomenon can be attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds. B-TWF exhibits similar tensile properties to TWF, with a tensile strength of 22.7 MPa and elongation at break of 12.6 %. However, the tensile properties of Y-TWF are different. In contrast to TWF, the cross-linking within Y-TWF is interspersed, resulting in Y-TWF exhibiting a tensile strength of 39.5 MPa and an elongation at a break of 7 %.

In Fig. 6(b), the Young's modulus of NW is 4.2 GPa, while that of LW is 2.1 GPa. This indicates that the modification of lignin has rendered LW more fragile [49]. PVA has a Young's modulus of 0.5 GPa, while TWF has a Young's modulus of 7.1 GPa, which is 1.7 times that of NW and 3.4 times that of LW. B-TWF and TWF have the similar tensile strength and Young's modulus. Young's modulus of B-TWF is 7.7 GPa. However, Y-TWF has Young's modulus of 17.5 GPa, which is 2.5 times higher than that of TWF and 4.2 times higher than that of NW. The tensile strength of Y-TWF is 14.1 times higher than that of NW, which is a significant improvement in comparison. The flexibilities of B-TWF and Y-TWF are illustrated in Fig. 6(c), which depict that B-TWF can be essentially restored to its original state after 5 min of bending. This is attributed to the flexibility imparted by its high break elongation of 12.4 %. Similarly, following a 5 min period, the Y-TWF is observed to have nearly recovered to its original state. When placed on a flat surface, they will recover within 10 min.

From Fig. 6(d), the WCA of NW after oven drying was 66.5°, while the WCA of LW after freeze drying was 17°, which was superior to NW in terms of hydrophilicity. This was due to the partial removal of lignin, which exposed the -OH in cellulose, rendering it more hydrophilic [50]. Consequently, the LW would be more readily impregnated with PVA. The WCA of PVA was measured to be 31.5°, which is due to the large number of hydrophilic groups -OH in PVA. The WCA of TWF was 27°. After combining LW with PVA, the cellulose still has a high degree of hydrophilicity. Because of the high water-conducting capacity of PVA, its WCA is relatively low. B-TWF also exhibits similar characteristics. The water-solubility of the BCDs results in minimal change in the WCA. Consequently, the WCA of B-TWF is 28.5°. However, the WCA of Y-TWF was 60° due to the cross-linking of the YCDs with -OH groups on cellulose, which resulted in a reduction of hydrophilic functional groups. Additionally, the presence of hydrophobic long alkyl chains and aromatic rings in the YCDs contributed to a higher WCA of Y-TWF compared to TWF and B-TWF [33].

As illustrated in Fig. 6(e), the mass of dried NW increased 4 times after being immersed in deionized water for one hour. This is attributed to the porous structure of NW, which facilitates water absorption [51], and the mass remained largely unchanged after 2 h of dissolution. The changing tendency of PVA was comparable to that of NW. The mass of PVA increased by 1.8 times after 2 h. The changing curves of TWF and B-TWF exhibited similar patterns. The swelling curves of TWF and B-TWF exhibited a near-identical profile, indicating that the hydrophilic BCDs had no discernible impact on the swelling of TWF. After 2 h of swelling, their mass increased 0.91 times and 0.97 times, respectively. However, there was a discrepancy in the swelling curves of Y-TWF, in which the hydrophilicity of cellulose was diminished due to a reduction in the number of hydroxyl (-OH) groups, resulting in a slower increase in mass within the first hour [52]. Furthermore, the mass also increased by 0.9 times after 2 h. It can be observed that NW exhibits a robust water absorption capacity, which will be advantageous in absorbing hydrophilic PVA. The incorporation of BCDs does not appear to influence the absorption of PVA by NW, whereas the introduction of YCDs appears to exert a certain degree of impediment on the wettability properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and UV-blocking properties of Fl-TWF
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Fig. 7(a), a broad absorption band at 3300 cm−1 is observed for BCDs, which is attributed to the substantial amount of -COOH in BCDs. Additionally, there is a stretching vibration peak of CC at 1620 cm−1, a stretching vibration peak of CN at 1360 cm−1, and a stretching vibration peak of C-O-C at 1109 cm−1 and 1192 cm−1 [53,54]. The B-TWF spectrum exhibits a paucity of peaks characteristic of BCDs, likely due to the low concentration of BCDs in the sample. However, a peak at 1340 cm−1 is observed, which can be attributed to the successful cross-linking of BCDs. From the FTIR spectrum of YCDs, it can be observed that there is a stretching vibration peak at 3450 cm−1, which is attributed to NH [55]. Additionally, there is a stretching vibration peak of NH2 at 3385 cm−1. Furthermore, there is an absorption peak at 1634 cm−1, which is attributed to CN, indicating the presence of pyridine N in YCDs [56]. Additionally, there is an absorption peak at 1458 cm−1, which is attributed to CN, indicating the presence of pyrrole N in YCDs [57]. In Y-TWF, due to the low content of YCDs, there is a broad absorption band at 3452 cm−1, which is caused by -OH in cellulose and PVA, and the presence of CN functional groups in YCDs at 1634 cm−1, and CN functional groups at 1458 cm−1 [56], which indicates that the YCDs were successfully cross-linked.

From Fig. 7(b), TWF had a mean transmittance of 72.2 % at the wavelength range of 400–800 nm, and exhibited a UV-blocking rate of 29.5 %, which was better than the pure PVA film (20.0 %) as presented in Fig. 7(d). This is because the lignin in wood has UV-blocking property [58]. Although the chromogenic groups of lignin in wood were removed after modification, the modified lignin in LW still retained its ability to absorb UV light, thereby exhibiting certain UV-blocking property, which has been testified in our previous work [15]. In comparison to TWF, B-TWF exhibits an average transmittance of 65.8 % at the wavelength range of 400–800 nm, displaying a slightly reduced transmittance. This is attributed to the incorporation of BCDs, which results in a color difference and a decline in brightness. Additionally, the absorption of UV light by the BCDs contributes to an enhanced blocking ability in the UV band. The average transmittance of Y-TWF at the wavelength range of 400–800 nm is 64.8 %, while its blocking rates at the wavelength ranges of 200–315 nm (UV-B) and 315–400 nm (UV-A) are 92.7 % and 60 %, respectively. The blocking rates of B-TWF at UV-B and UV-A bands are 74.5 % and 48.1 %, respectively. Their absorptions towards UV light might be due to π → π* leaps in the CC bond and n → π* leaps in the CO bond in CDs. Consequently, Y-TWF exhibits both excellent optical transmittance and high UV-blocking capability.

The schematic diagram as presented in Fig. 7(c) illustrated the UV-blocking mechanism. The amplitude of B-TWF and Y-TWF were measured by the UV lamp and amplitude counter, in which the power of the UV lamp was 50 W. The UV lamp was placed on top of the wood slice, and the amplitude counter was placed on the bottom of the wood slice. The UV-blocking rates of different samples were calculated. When TWF was placed in the middle of the UV lamp and amplitude counter, the value of the amplitude counter was 0.705 times of the Blank. Therefore, the UV-blocking of TWF was 29.5 %. The UV-blocking rates of B-TWF-5, B-TWF-4, B-TWF-3, B-TWF-2, and B-TWF-1 were measured to be 88.6 %, 83.5 %, 82.5 %, 80.2 % and 73.2 %, respectively (Fig. 7(d)). The UV-blocking rates of Y-TWF-25, Y-TWF-20, Y-TWF-15, Y-TWF-10 and Y-TWF-5 were measured to be 94.8 %, 93.7 %, 92.4 %, 87.4 % and 85.7 %, respectively (Fig. 7(e)). Then the amplitude counter was replaced with a UV sensor card to visualize the UV-blocking capability of different samples (Fig. 7(f)). The white UV sensor card changed to purple after being exposed to UV light. The darker the purple color, the higher the intensity of UV light exposure [59]. Apparently, the UV-blocking capability of Y-TWF is significantly higher than B-TWF in the actual situation.

The L* (luminance), a* (red/green) and b* (yellow/blue) values of B-TWF and Y-TWF were evaluated. The L*, a* and b* values of TWF are 81.89, 0.56 and 0.32, respectively. However, the addition of BCDs affects the color of B-TWF. The more the BCDs content, the lower the L* value, the higher the a* and b* values. As the content of BCDs increases, ∆E also increases (Fig. 7(g)), and the color difference with TWF is larger. Similarly, more content of YCDs would be adsorbed by LW with the increasing impregnation time, and the ∆E difference of Y-TWF to TWF will be larger (Fig. 7(h)). In conclusion, TWF exhibits excellent light transmittance, which is diminished following the successful cross-linking of BCDs and YCDs. However, the UV-blocking capacity is markedly enhanced. Among them, increasing the content of BCDs improved the UV-blocking capability of B-TWF, but its UV-blocking capability was limited compared with Y-TWF. To Y-TWF, the variations in UV-blocking capacity and chromatic aberration diagram after impregnation YCDs solution for 15 min are small. Thus, the mass ratio of PVA: BCDs: deionized water of 100:3:1000 was determined to be the optimal ratio for the preparation of B-TWF. The most optimal impregnation time with YCDs solution was determined to be 15 min for fabricating B-TWF.

Window is an essential component of any residential structure, providing access to interior space with natural light. Moreover, it impacts the aesthetic and function of the architecture. We test by tightly covering the inside of the glass window with TWF/Fl-TWF. Researches have demonstrated that Fl-TWF has unique light scattering properties [60]. The diffusion effect of Y-TWF is especially pronounced, exhibiting the greater diffusion area and intensity than TWF and B-TWF. The passage of sunlight through a window results in the formation of a rectangular shadow on the ground (Fig. 8(a)). However, when TWF itself or co-worked with glass was applied to the window as illustrated in Fig. 8(b), light scattering will occur [61]. To observe this phenomenon, a box (100 × 100 × 200 mm3) with a hole (10 × 10 mm2) on its top was constructed from a cardboard box. Under sunlight, a rectangle is observed at the bottom of box (Fig. 8(c)), just like the ordinary glass served as a window (Fig. 8(d)). When simulated sunlight passed through TWF (Fig. 8(e)), light would be scattered within TWF (due to its distinctive fiber structure), thereby illuminating a larger area. The illuminating area of B-TWF reduced, but was still larger than the ordinary glass. This is because the absorption spectrum of BCDs (Fig. 2(f)) demonstrates that they can absorb a considerable quantity of visible light in addition to UV light. Consequently, B-TWF exhibits greater light absorption than TWF, which leads to a reduction in the area and intensity of the scattered light [62]. From the absorption spectrum of YCDs (Fig. 3(f)), YCDs absorb ultraviolet light and convert it into yellow fluorescence. Compared to TWF, the scattered light of Y-TWF appears pale yellow with a larger area and higher brightness.

To quantify the scattering capability of TWF, the directions along and perpendicular to its fiber growth were defined as the coordinates x and y, respectively. As seen in Fig. 8(h), when a red laser passed through the TWF, the laser exhibited a scattering phenomenon. The scattering intensity was calculated for angle range between −30° and 30° [61]. Specifically, the attenuation of scattering intensity along the x direction is faster, which complete disappears at a scattering angle of 20°. In contrast, the attenuation of scattering intensity along the y direction is slower, which complete disappears at a scattering angle of 25°. As illustrated in Fig. 8(i), the light scattering phenomenon of B-YWF is comparable to TWF, while its light scattering area reduced. In the x direction, the light intensity of B-TWF at a scattering angle of ±8.9° decreased to 10 % of its center, while the light intensity of TWF decreased to 10 % of its center at a scattering angle of ±7.4°. However, the BCDs absorb the light, resulting in a light intensity reduction in the central of B-YWF. Although the attenuation of scattering intensity was slower than TWF, the resulting light intensity was insufficient to produce the same scattering area as TWF (see the insert in Fig. 8(i)). The center light scattering intensity of Y-TWF was almost identical to TWF. However, its attenuation of scattering intensity was slower than TWF in the x-direction (Fig. 8(j)). The light intensity of Y-TWF decreased to 10 % of its center at a scattering angle of ±11.1°. Consequently, the scattering area of Y-TWF was larger in comparison to TWF. The same is true for the y-direction.

As illustrated in Fig. 8(f), B-TWF exhibits a slight reduction in transmittance under visible light, and displays a brown color compared to TWF (without any color). Y-TWF demonstrates a comparable transmittance to B-TWF, and displays a yellow color under visible light. Under UV irradiation, TWF with good transmittance did not show any fluorescence, while B-TWF and Y-TWF emitted bright blue and yellow fluorescence, respectively. Meantime, the words under B-TWF and Y-TWF could be easily read with their fluorescence (Fig. 8(g)), indicating the good transmittance and fluorescence of Fl-TWF samples fabricated in this work. To summarize, the capabilities to scatter light and convert UV light make Fl-TWF samples great potential for window applications. Furthermore, the UV-blocking capacity of Fl-TWF samples offers enhanced protection for the skin, eyes, and so on [63,64]. Notably, YCDs encapsulated in Y-TWF can absorb the blue-violet band of sunlight and convert it into yellow fluorescence. Under identical light condition, Y-TWF exhibited larger scattering area and greater light intensity. Consequently, Y-TWF was more suitable for window applications. The following tests will be focused on Y-TWF for evaluating its practical applicability.

The impact of relative humidity (RH) on building and decorative materials is a complex and multifaceted issue containing the physical and chemical attributes. As presented in Fig. 9 (a-d), Y-TWF samples were respectively placed in the closed containers (with constant the RHs of 20, 40, 60 and 80 % inside) containing the electronic hygrometers. Another Y-TWF sample was placed outside with the RH of 35–40 %. After 24 h of treatment, Y-TWF samples were pick out of the containers, which as well as the one outside were subsequently measured and analyzed on their variations in the aspects of mass, tensile strength, fluorescence intensity and UV blocking rate. From Fig. 9(a), we could find that the fluorescence intensity of Y-TWF was enhanced along with the increasing RH. As shown in Fig. 9(e), the mass of Y-TWF decreased by 0.7 % at the RH of 20 %. This is because the moisture in Y-TWF (RH: 35–40 %) escaped to its exterior (in the closed container at lower RH). The tensile strength of Y-TWF could be further improved when its surrounding RH was lower than the normal external environment. At the RH of 20 %, the tensile strength of Y-TWF reach up to 49.38 MPa (Fig. 9(f)). Conversely, when the RH in the closed container was higher than the outside, the higher RH the greater mass of Y-TWF. During the increasing RH from 40 % to 80 % in the closed container, the mass of Y-TWF increased by 0.8 % to 5.4 %. At this point, the tensile strength of Y-TWF floated around 40 MPa. Notably, it was almost same as the Y-TWF placed outside (39.5 MPa) (Fig. S6 in Supplementary Material), which fully demonstrated the highly stable cross-linking between PVA and Y-LW (main compound is cellulose). As illustrated in Fig. 9(g), all the UV-blocking rates of Y-TWF samples after treatment at different RHs for 34 h exceeded 91 %, suggesting its excellent UV resistance stability.

Except for the evaluation under high relative humidity, a further discussion of the water stability of Y-TWF after immersing treatment for 2 h is warranted. Fig. 9(h) showed the optical photographs of Y-TWF under visible and UV light in original, swelling and recovery states. After reaching saturation, the tensile strength of Y-TWF was evaluated as presented in Fig. 9(i). At this point, it was 21.64 MPa, representing a 45.1 % decline in comparison to the original. Then this Y-TWF sample was left to naturally air-drying. Two hours later, the tensile strength of the recovery Y-TWF sample reached 36.31 MPa, which was 92 % of the original (Fig. S7 in Supplementary Material). Notably, Y-TWF has the photoluminescence characteristics throughout the whole procedure. In conclusion, Y-TWF did not undergo drastic alteration in the conditions of high RH and water immersing, exhibiting a robust stability. Furthermore, Y-TWF was subjected to heating on an electric hot plate (Fig. S8(a) in Supplementary Material). After 180 s of heating, the temperature of background rapidly reached 100 °C. Concurrently, Y-TWF reached 83.5 °C at 180 s, continued to heat up (90 °C) for an additional 80s, and stabilized gradually, which was much lower than the background (reaching 104 °C at 260 s). Apparently, Y-TWF exhibited a slower heating rate compared to the background, and reached a maximum temperature which was always lower than the ambient temperature (Fig. S8(b) in Supplementary Material). Notably, the size and color of Y-TWF did not change significantly during the whole process. These findings suggested that Y-TW exhibited excellent heat preservation, insulation capabilities and structural durability at high temperature, which suggested the good adaptability and potential of Y-TWF applying to the windows of building.

The durability and expansibility of building and decorative material are of paramount importance for its intended application. The durability of Y-TWF has been evaluated by long-term UV exposure in a photoaging chamber. Initially, Y-TWF was cut into the small slices for reducing the error caused by the difference in photoaging time. Subsequently, the transparency, fluorescence, tensile strength and UV-blocking rate after different photoaging time (12, 24, 36, 48, 60 and 72 h) were carefully compared. As presented in Fig. 10(a), the extending photoaging period resulted in the color burn and fluorescence intensity reduction of Y-TWF in a small range. There was no dimensional shrinkage, cracking and other adverse conditions happened on Y-TWF. This is because PVA under UV irradiation will undergo degradation, which can be significantly mitigated through the cross-linking of cellulose and PVA [65]. The addition of YCDs could absorb the majority of UV light, which would reduce the degradation of PVA. Moreover, the UV irradiation could promote the further cross-linking between cellulose, PVA and YCDs. Therefore, the tensile strength and UV resistance of Y-TWF were improved as the photoaging time prolonged. As shown in Fig. 10(b), its tensile strength was enhanced rapidly from 39.5 MPa to 63.5 MPa after 72 h of photoaging treatment (Fig. S9 in Supplementary Material). Meantime, the UV-blocking rate of Y-TWF was improved tardily from 92.41 % to 95.86 % as provided in Fig. 10(c). As the photoaging time increased, the UV resistance of Y-TWF gradually remained constant, indicating that the components in Y-TWF could be resistant to degradation. In conclusion, Y-TWF exhibited the good durability, UV and degradation resistance against photoaging treatment. Coupled with its good water stability, Y-TWF could be applied to most life scenarios.

To enlarge the size of Y-TWF, its expansibility has been investigated scientifically. The use of PVA allowed for the splicing of Y-TWF samples to form a larger Y-TWF. As illustrated in Fig. 10(d-e), two Y-TWF samples were placed on a slide, the gap between which was filled with PVA for splicing. After 12 h of natural air-drying, a spliced Y-TWF was obtained (see Fig. 10(f)), thereby enabling the preparation of Y-TWF with larger size. Besides, the spliced Y-TWF exhibited no discernible alteration from the original except for a very narrow gap at the point of splicing. Notably, the tensile strength of spliced Y-TWF at the splicing point could be comparable to original Y-TWF (see Fig. 10(h)). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of the spliced Y-TWF (see Fig. 10 (g)) has not been affected under ultraviolet light, thereby further expanding its potential applications.

Developing novel environmentally friendly building and decorative materials has become a significant research focus in the field of material science, particularly in light of the mounting pressure on the global environment and resources. The emergence of Fl-TWF represents an innovative solution to address the environmental burden caused by the production and use of petroleum-based materials. In this work, LW was obtained by the lignin modification of balsa wood. B-TWF and Y-TWF were obtained by impregnating LW with as-prepared BCDs/YCDs and PVA in two different preparation methods. The light transmittance (65.8 % and 64.8 %), tensile strength (22.7 MPa and 39.5 MPa), UV-blocking rate (82.5 % and 92.4 %) and photoluminescent property (blue and yellow fluorescence) of B-TWF and Y-TWF were compared. Results showed that Y-TWF exhibited greater tensile strength, UV-blocking rate, and the capability of blue-violet light conversion, rendering it particularly advantageous for window applications. To evaluate the long-term stability, the performances of Y-TW in humid and photoaging chambers were investigated, whose results demonstrated that the mutual cross-linking of YCDs, PVA, and LW rendered the entire structure highly stable. As the RH increased, the fluorescence intensity of Y-TWF enhanced, and its tensile strength remained stable. However, the low RH (below daily humidity) brought a tensile strength increase of Y-TWF, while there was a notable decline in its fluorescence intensity. Notably, the UV-blocking rate of Y-TWF remained unaltered across all RH levels. After immersing to water for 2 h, the tensile strength of Y-TWF decreased by 45 %, while its mechanical property could recovery 92 % of the initial after natural air-drying for 2 h. Moreover, with prolonged UV irradiation in photoaging chamber, the UV resistance of Y-TWF remained stable, meanwhile, its tensile strength increased. More importantly, a couple of Y-TWF samples can be spliced by PVA to form a larger Y-TWF, displaying an excellent expansibility. Apparently, Y-TWF is a green, stable and reliable material for long-term building and decorative use. The application of this solution to the interior of glass windows has been demonstrated to be an effective solution to the problems posed by direct sunlight and excessive UV rays. However, the material does possess certain limitations, including reduced light transmittance in comparison to glass (~90 %) and susceptibility to surface damage. Consequently, there is a necessity to further investigate methods to augment the transmittance (e.g., enhancing the density of Fl-TWF through pressurization) and optimize the preparation process to enhance the wear resistance. Further research and verification are necessary to ascertain its stabilities in mass production, cost control, and practical application.

Ming Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Wangchun Gou: Visualization, Validation. Zhenghui Li: Visualization, Data curation. Dongsheng Song: Validation, Data curation.
